summary: The chapter continues with two major topics. First: a halakhic principle that Jerusalem (and the Temple) does not bring the egla arufa (calf atonement for an unknown murder) - with the underlying principle that the city's sanctity is incompatible with that ritual's presupposition of unsolved crime. Second: the detailed laws of the ash removal (terumat hadeshen) - how much ash must be removed, where it is placed, what garments the priest wears, and whether the ash of the outer altar differs from the ash of the menorah.

display.title: Why Jerusalem is exempt from the unsolved-murder ritual
display.whats: Jerusalem does not bring the egla arufa (the calf slaughtered in a valley for an unresolved murder). The source: the verse 'you shall measure to the cities' - Jerusalem cannot be 'measured to,' either because it was not divided among the tribes (and thus has no 'city' owner) or because the Temple's presence makes the notion of unresolved guilt incompatible with the city's identity.
display.hint: The egla arufa ritual assumes an unsolved murder near a city whose inhabitants bear some collective guilt. Jerusalem's unique status - undivided among tribes, the city of the divine Presence - makes this ritual categorically inapplicable. The city of God cannot implicitly confess collective guilt over an unsolved murder.

learning.learnerQuestion: Every Israelite city that finds an unidentified murder victim nearby must perform the egla arufa atonement ritual. Why is Jerusalem the one city explicitly excused?
learning.coreTension: The egla arufa's mechanism only works if there is a single defined city to be 'measured to' as the nearest one to the body, since that city bears the ritual obligation. Jerusalem was deliberately never allocated to any one tribe, so the very anchor the ritual's measurement requires does not exist there.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara gives two independent grounds for the exemption. Textually, the verse's 'measure to the cities' language presupposes a city with a tribal owner, and Jerusalem has none. Substantively, even setting the measurement technicality aside, a ritual that publicly declares unresolved communal guilt is incompatible with the sanctity of the city housing the divine Presence.
learning.resolution: Jerusalem is exempt from the egla arufa on two independent grounds: it has no tribal owner to be measured to, and a ritual premised on unresolved communal guilt is incompatible with the Temple's sanctity.

takeaway.text: A ritual obligation that depends on a specific legal category (here, tribal city ownership) does not apply where that category cannot be established, even if the underlying situation the ritual addresses could still occur.

learning.ahaMoment: The egla arufa assumes: someone was killed; no one knows who did it; the nearest city bears some collective responsibility. Jerusalem's exemption says: Jerusalem has no ownership by any tribe, and the divine Presence there is incompatible with unresolved guilt. The city of atonement cannot itself need atonement through an unsolved murder ritual.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume Jerusalem's exemption means murders there never went unsolved. The sugya does not address what, if anything, provided atonement for an unidentified killer within Jerusalem itself; it only establishes that this specific ritual is technically and substantively inapplicable to the city.
learning.memoryAnchor: Jerusalem: no egla arufa. Not divided among tribes, so no owner city to measure to. Also: city of God cannot ritually admit unresolved collective guilt.

argumentFlow.label: Jerusalem does not bring egla arufa
argumentFlow.text: Jerusalem (and the Temple Mount) is exempt from the egla arufa obligation that applies to cities near which an unidentified murder victim is found.

argumentFlow.label: Source: 'you shall measure to the cities'
argumentFlow.text: The verse refers to measuring the distance to determine which city is closest. Jerusalem cannot be 'measured to' because it was never allocated to any specific tribe - it belongs to all Israel (or to God). Therefore the measurement does not apply.

argumentFlow.label: Additional reason: Temple sanctity incompatible
argumentFlow.text: Even if one argued Jerusalem could be measured to, the Temple's presence makes the egla arufa ritual inappropriate. The city of atonement cannot itself be accused of collective guilt.

theological: Jerusalem as city of God - not a tribal city

misconceptions.misconception: Jerusalem's exemption from egla arufa means the ritual is treating Jerusalem as morally superior or incapable of having unsolved murders.
misconceptions.correction: The exemption is categorical and definitional: Jerusalem does not meet the ritual's requirement of a tribally-owned 'nearest city,' not a statement about Jerusalem's moral standing.

misconceptions.misconception: Jerusalem not being divided among the tribes is a minor administrative detail with no legal consequence.
misconceptions.correction: It is a deliberate, distinct legal fact (unlike every other city, which was allocated to a specific tribe) with ongoing halachic implications, including this exemption.

visualizableElements.description: Normal city: divided among tribes, can be 'measured to' for egla arufa. Jerusalem: undivided, belongs to all Israel, exempt from the egla arufa ritual.
The egla arufa ritual in a valley outside a normal city - showing what Jerusalem is explicitly exempt from

quizSeeds.question: Why does Jerusalem not bring the egla arufa when an unidentified murder victim is found?
quizSeeds.answer: Jerusalem was never allocated to any specific tribe. The egla arufa requires identifying the 'nearest city' to the victim by tribal measurement, and Jerusalem cannot be measured to in the required sense because it has no tribal owner. The Temple's sanctity gives a second, independent reason.

quizSeeds.question: What two independent reasons does the Gemara give for Jerusalem's egla arufa exemption?
quizSeeds.answer: Textually, Jerusalem has no tribal owner to be measured to. Substantively, a ritual that declares unresolved communal guilt is incompatible with the sanctity of the city housing the divine Presence.

sugyot.finalRuling: Jerusalem does not bring the egla arufa: it was not divided among the tribes and its sanctity is incompatible with the ritual.

glossary.en: The calf whose neck is broken in a valley as atonement for an unresolved murder; Jerusalem is exempt from this obligation.

display.whats: The laws of terumat hadeshen (ash removal) in detail: the priest wears smaller, worn-out versions of his regular four garments (not new ones). He removes a full fistful of ash from inside the heap and places it beside the altar ramp on the east side. The removal is a mitzvah even if the priest uses his left hand. The ash of the outer altar vs. the ash of the menorah: what does one do with each type? Discussion of where the ashes must be deposited.
display.hint: The requirement to wear 'worn' garments for the ash removal service is counterintuitive - normally priestly service demands the finest. The reason given: one should not wear one's best clothes while cleaning. This preserves the dignity of the fine garments for the main service.

learning.summary: For terumat hadeshen the priest wears four special garments - smaller, worn versions of the regular four priestly garments, so as not to soil the fine garments with ash. He removes a fistful of ash from the top of the altar heap. The ash is placed on the east side of the altar ramp. It is a mitzvah even if done with the left hand. The ash of the outer altar is placed beside the ramp; the ash of the inner altar (ketoret) is placed in a specific location. The menorah ash goes outside the Temple entirely.
learning.ahaMoment: The worn-garment rule is a lesson about proportionality: dress for the task. The job of removing ash is not glamorous - wear the appropriate garments. Save the fine garments for the fine service. This is not diminishing the ash removal; it is placing everything in its proper context.
learning.memoryAnchor: Ash removal: worn garments (not best). Fistful of ash. East of ramp. Left hand acceptable. Outer altar ash = ramp side; menorah ash = outside.
learning.openQuestion: Why is the left hand acceptable for ash removal when most Temple services require the right? Is ash removal considered less formally 'sacred' than the main offerings?

argumentFlow.label: Priest wears worn garments for ash removal
argumentFlow.text: For terumat hadeshen, the priest does not wear his standard fine four garments but instead smaller, worn-out versions of the same four, to avoid soiling the fine garments with ash.

argumentFlow.label: Procedure: remove a fistful of ash from the top
argumentFlow.text: The priest takes a full fistful of ash from the top of the pile that accumulated on the altar during the night. The quantity is significant: a full fistful, not more.

argumentFlow.label: Placement: east side of the altar ramp
argumentFlow.text: The ash is carried down the ramp and placed beside it on the eastern side, where it dissolves/absorbs into the ground. The ash is not carried outside the Temple courtyard.

argumentFlow.label: Left hand acceptable for ash removal
argumentFlow.text: Unlike most Temple services which require the right hand, ash removal is valid even if performed with the left hand. This reflects its different halakhic character.

argumentFlow.label: Ash of the outer altar: beside the ramp
argumentFlow.text: The ash from the outer (burnt offering) altar is deposited on the east side of the altar ramp, where it is absorbed into the ground.

argumentFlow.label: Ash of the inner altar (ketoret): specific location
argumentFlow.text: The ash from the inner altar (where incense was burned) is placed in a different location - a clean space near the altar but specifically designated.

argumentFlow.label: Ash from the menorah: taken outside
argumentFlow.text: The ash from cleaning the menorah is taken outside the Temple entirely - to a clean place outside.

argumentFlow.label: Why different rules for each type of ash?
argumentFlow.text: Each sacred object's ash must be treated in a manner appropriate to its sanctity. The outer altar ash stays inside the courtyard; the inner altar and menorah ash have different destinations.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration on the worn garments rule
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara further elaborates why worn garments are required: a proverb 'do not use the garments in which you cook for your master to pour wine for him' - different tasks deserve different dress.

argumentFlow.label: Further garment details
argumentFlow.text: The worn garments were still valid priestly garments - they were not disqualified, just lesser quality. The requirement was that the priest not soil his best garments.

argumentFlow.label: Minimum quantity of ash
argumentFlow.text: The fistful of ash is the minimum. If the priest removed less than a fistful, he has not fulfilled the mitzvah. The fistful must come from the actual accumulated ash, not from the sides.

argumentFlow.label: Summary of terumat hadeshen laws
argumentFlow.text: The ash removal service is a complete mitzvah with its own garment rules, quantity requirements, location requirements, and hand preference (though left is valid). Its detailed laws show it is a full priestly service despite being unglamous.

halachic: three types of ash with different destinations
theological: every Temple act is a complete mitzvah

misconceptions: The worn garments are still kosher priestly garments - not ordinary clothes. They are proper Temple vestments, just not the finest ones. The priest is still fully dressed for service.
Left hand validity does not make ash removal 'less' of a mitzvah - it simply reflects a different halachic category where right-hand specificity was not included in the mitzvah's definition.

visualizableElements.description: A priest in worn (but valid) priestly garments taking a fistful of ash from the top of the altar heap
The east side of the altar ramp where the daily ash from the outer altar is deposited and absorbed into the ground
Three ash types and their destinations: outer altar ash (east of ramp), inner altar ash (designated clean area), menorah ash (outside Temple entirely)

quizSeeds.q: What garments does a priest wear for the ash removal service, and why?
quizSeeds.a: Worn-out versions of the four standard priestly garments - so as not to soil the fine garments with ash. A proverb: 'do not use the garments in which you cook for your master to pour wine for him.'

quizSeeds.q: What are the three types of Temple ash and where is each deposited?
quizSeeds.a: (1) Outer altar ash: east side of the altar ramp inside the courtyard. (2) Inner altar (ketoret) ash: a specific clean designated area. (3) Menorah ash: taken outside the Temple entirely to a clean place.

quizSeeds.q: Can ash removal be performed with the left hand?
quizSeeds.a: Yes. Unlike most Temple services that require the right hand, ash removal is valid even with the left hand.

sugyot.finalRuling: Ash removal requires worn priestly garments, a full fistful of ash from the altar top, placed east of the ramp. Left hand valid. Different ash types go to different locations.

glossary.en: Worn priestly garments; used for the ash removal service to spare the fine garments from soiling.
A full fistful; the minimum amount of ash that must be removed to fulfill the mitzvah of terumat hadeshen.
Calf atonement ritual for unresolved murder; Jerusalem is exempt.
Worn priestly garments for ash removal; spare the fine garments for main service.
Full fistful; minimum ash quantity for terumat hadeshen.
